author: @climatebabes
text: MIT used 10 Sun. You can actually make steam with 1 Sun if you create a blackened alu sheet floating on water with tiny holes at 5cm intervals or so. The metal will collect the heat and dump it on the water that wicks through the hole..

author: @PressToDigitate
text: Robert, all that exquisitely conductive Carbon Foam/Exfoliated Graphite and the rising steam just shouts out "Charge Separation".  Substantial voltage could be achieved with the right configuration between two evaporation streams. Combined with "Lord Kelvin's Water Dropping Condenser", on the down cycle, an interesting and practical desalination apparatus might be constructed.  With charge-sep on both evaporation and condensation, enough static electricity could be captured/released/generated to charge [your] supercapacitors, thence a battery, which then performs useful functions.  This could be a USB cellphone/device charger, or overnight LED illumination for remote village environments, or a small aquarium pump for moving/dispensing the water, or a solar tracking motor for a lens/reflector, etc.  Water *AND* Power, anywhere under the sun, with ultracheap environmental materials, now that's something you might find immediate funding for, just based on the prototype (from the GEF and other NGOs, among others).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is funny mate - i never thought about that - that is a very interesting idea - thanks for taking the time to share

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: Seems like a thin layer of your ink could go between the wick and graphite if you have a formulation that dries with a decent porosity and resistivity. That way it could be a hybrid system where the sun could be utilized to its full extent, and if needed supplimental power could be passed through the ink layer to boost the heat. The wick could be a half inch of untreated rockwool with the fibers vertical with a bit of untreated closed cell foam underneath to handle buoyancy. Some holes through the foam could ensure even wicking, but rockwood wicks amazingly so I don't think it would need anything but the sides exposed for areas under a square foot or two.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice ideas mate - cheers

author: @scotttovey
text:
Cool tech Robert.

To utilize this for water collection; one would need to build a glass top that funneled the steam into a tube which ports it to a stainless steel collector such as would be used to collect water from the dew at night.

The bottom should have a grid that the carbonized foam sits on and allows the water to come in contact with the foam. Adding a floating ring around the outside of the container would allow the container to float in the water. Adding a second container next to that in which the steam was funneled, would make a compact water collection device.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: or polypropylene mate?

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
Over time, the polypropylene will deteriorate whereas glass will not.
The plastic may also cloud up over time preventing the sunlight from heating the graphene.
For a long term use, I think glass is the better material. 
Either way, it will need to be cleaned from time to time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @scotttovey cheers mate

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering 😎👍

author: @benthere8051
text:
How does this help generate steam? Does the carbon absorb solar energy so well that steam is generated at the surface only?  I take it that the whole body of water does not have to reach 100 C, just the water at the surface of the carbon? It would take very much less energy to produce water vapor if that is the case.
If the resultant water vapor was ducted through tubes just below the surface of the carbon, the heat of vaporization could be recycled into the water about to be vaporized. The same heat could be used twice, or many times for that matter.

author: @Barskor1
text:
A simple set up to use the oasis graphene foam to distill water.
Get a fish aquarium a high sided tray it will fit in and a glass baking tray to fit inside the aquarium.
place big tray down fill the baking tray with seawater and the foam place aquarium over the baking tray place it all in open sunlight.

As an unemployed housedad without a shop I live vicariously through your videos 😎

Can you investigate a possible future experiment with your graphene kung-fu?

@ThinkingandTinkering no rush.

And the part in this video where you mentioned steam production for an engine, well hold on to your hat for this tidbit.

If you take a high-pressure injector out of a diesel engine and modify it to squirt salt water at a stainless steel impacting surface at close range, the cavitation of all those tiny droplets makes some devilry take place.

Basically a very VERY big boom of flash steam 🤩

author: @jeanmichel4489
text: Robert, what is oasis foam?

replies.author: @sislertx
replies.text: There are 2 types..both used in floristy...one that is for water..one is for silk flowers...usually found at craft stores

replies.author: @jeanmichel4489
replies.text:
@sislertx 
Many thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Sisterx has it mate - but just to add Oasis is a trademarked name for wet floral foam, the spongy phenolic foam used for real flower arranging. It soaks up water like a sponge and acts both as a preservative to prolong the life of the flowers and a support to hold them in place. The foam's structure is similar to that of plants and has capillary action to move water to the surface and up the stem. It is often green, but is also available in many other colours, such as purple, red, yellow, and brown. It usually is supplied in a brick shape - straight from wikipaedia mate - cheers

replies.author: @jeanmichel4489
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering

I've seen a cylinder in your workshop. I do not know if it is oxygen or acetylene but it makes me remember that oxygen is the real enemy for all diseases. If you have oxygen use it to breath sometimes two times a week,  for instance.
Do it man! 
Regards.
